material: You are a physician at a community health clinic. Sarah, a 14-year-old patient, comes to your office alone requesting birth control pills. She is articulate and composed but visibly nervous. She tells you she has a 17-year-old boyfriend and they have been sexually active for two months. She specifically asks that you not tell her parents, who she describes as "very religious" and says would "kick her out of the house" if they found out. In your jurisdiction, minors have the legal right to confidential reproductive health care. However, you notice that Sarah seems anxious beyond what you would expect, and when you ask about her relationship, she hesitates before saying "everything is fine."
text: How would you handle this situation? What are the ethical considerations?

material: Your close friend and fellow medical student, James, age 26, confides in you late one evening that he has been struggling with severe depression for the past six months. He has been self-medicating with prescription opioids that he took from a hospital supply cabinet during his clinical rotation in the emergency department. He says the drugs are the only thing that help him sleep. James is currently on a paediatrics rotation and is responsible for patient care. He begs you not to tell anyone, saying that reporting it would end his medical career — and he is $280,000 in student debt. He promises to stop after exams, which are three weeks away. His hands are trembling as he talks to you.
text: What would you do? Walk through your reasoning.

material: Mrs. Margaret Chen, 72, has been your patient for eight years. She has stage IV pancreatic cancer that has metastasised to her liver and lungs. After two rounds of chemotherapy that caused severe nausea, fatigue, and neuropathy, she has decided to stop all treatment and enter palliative care. She is mentally competent and has documented her wishes in an advance directive. Her three adult children — David (45), Lisa (42), and Michael (38) — are united in opposing her decision. David, a lawyer, has told you directly that he will file a malpractice lawsuit if you "let their mother die." Lisa breaks down crying in your office daily. Margaret tells you privately: "I have made my peace. I want to die with dignity, not connected to machines."
text: How do you navigate this situation? What principles guide your decision?

material:
You are on a hospital ethics committee tasked with allocating one available liver. There are two patients:

Patient A: Maria Santos, 35, a single mother of three children (ages 4, 7, and 10). She has alcoholic liver disease and has been sober for 6 months after completing a residential treatment programme. Her counsellor reports she is highly motivated and has strong family support. Without a transplant, she has approximately 3 months to live. Her children are currently in the care of her elderly mother.

Patient B: Ethan Park, 12, who has a congenital biliary atresia that has progressively destroyed his liver despite two previous surgeries. He has been on the transplant list for 14 months. He is an otherwise healthy child who loves football and wants to be a veterinarian. Without a transplant, he has approximately 6 months to live. The next available liver from the donor registry may not come for 8-12 months.
text: How would you approach this decision? What factors should be considered?

material:
You are an early-career researcher at a university hospital. Meridian Pharmaceuticals, a multinational company, offers to fund your research lab with a $2 million grant over three years to study the efficacy of a new antidepressant. Your lab currently operates on a shoestring budget, and two of your postdoctoral researchers are about to lose their positions due to lack of funding.

The grant terms specify that Meridian will have the right to review all findings before publication and may delay publication by up to 6 months "to protect proprietary interests." Your department chair encourages you to accept, noting that Meridian has funded several other labs without incident. However, a colleague warns you that Meridian suppressed unfavourable data from a previous trial of a related drug, which only came to light through a whistleblower two years later.
text: Would you accept this offer? Discuss the ethical implications.

material:
You are a third-year medical student on your surgical rotation. During morning rounds, you witness Dr. Rebecca Torres, a senior resident, publicly berate Maria, a nurse of 15 years, in front of patients, families, and other staff. Dr. Torres shouts: "If you can't follow basic orders, maybe you should find another profession!" The issue was a 10-minute delay in administering a non-urgent medication. Maria's eyes fill with tears, but she says nothing and walks away.

Dr. Torres is widely regarded as one of the best surgical residents in the programme and is well-liked by the attending physicians. She is also the person who will be writing your rotation evaluation. Later, you overhear another nurse say, "This isn't the first time. We've all just learned to keep our heads down."
text: What would you do in this situation?

material:
A patient, Robert Kaminski, age 42, is a devout Jehovah's Witness brought to the emergency room after a serious car accident. He has lost approximately 40% of his blood volume and is in haemorrhagic shock. He is conscious, alert, and oriented, and he explicitly and repeatedly refuses a blood transfusion, citing his deeply held religious beliefs. He is wearing a medical alert bracelet that reads "No Blood" and carries a signed advance directive refusing transfusions.

His wife, Elena, who is not a Jehovah's Witness, is sobbing in the hallway and begging the surgical team to give the transfusion. "He has two young children," she pleads. "You cannot let him die over this." Robert's blood pressure is dropping, and the trauma surgeon estimates he has approximately 90 minutes before the blood loss becomes irreversible.
text: What do you do? Explain your reasoning.

material: The government is considering implementing a policy that would require all healthcare workers to be vaccinated against influenza annually, with no religious or philosophical exemptions, only medical exemptions.
text: What is your position on this policy? Discuss both sides of the argument.

material:
You are a third-year medical student observing an elective cholecystectomy (gallbladder removal). Forty-five minutes into the procedure, you notice that the attending surgeon, Dr. William Park, is making uncharacteristically imprecise movements. His speech is slightly slurred, and you detect what you think is the smell of alcohol. A scrub nurse catches your eye and gives you a barely perceptible head shake, as if to say "don't say anything."

Dr. Park is the chief of surgery and has been at the hospital for 22 years. He has an impeccable reputation. The patient is under general anaesthesia. The surgical fellow assisting seems nervous but is not speaking up. The procedure is approximately halfway complete, and abandoning it mid-operation carries its own risks.
text: What would you do? Consider immediate patient safety and longer-term concerns.

material: A couple comes to you for genetic counseling. Testing reveals that the fetus has Down syndrome. The couple asks for your personal recommendation on whether to continue or terminate the pregnancy.
text: How do you respond? What is the role of a physician in this situation?
What does empathy mean to you, and how have you demonstrated it in your life?
Describe a time when you had to advocate for someone who could not advocate for themselves.

material: Healthcare resources are limited. In a pandemic scenario, you must decide how to allocate 100 ventilators among 300 critically ill patients. You must establish allocation criteria.
text: What criteria would you use, and how would you justify your approach?

material: You are a family physician. A patient asks you to prescribe ivermectin for COVID-19, citing information they found on social media. Current evidence-based guidelines do not support this use.
text: How do you handle this conversation while maintaining the patient-physician relationship?

material: Role-play scenario: You will be speaking with a standardized patient. Mrs. Johnson is a 70-year-old woman who has just been told she needs hip replacement surgery. She is terrified of the procedure and wants to leave the hospital immediately.
text: Your task is to speak with Mrs. Johnson, address her concerns, and help her make an informed decision about her care.

material: An Indigenous community in a remote area has significantly worse health outcomes than the national average. The community has historically been distrustful of the healthcare system due to past abuses.
text: If you were tasked with improving healthcare delivery to this community, how would you approach it?
What do you think is the most pressing issue facing healthcare today, and what role can physicians play in addressing it?

material: A wealthy donor offers your hospital $10 million on the condition that the money be used exclusively to build a state-of-the-art cosmetic surgery wing. The hospital desperately needs funding for its emergency department and pediatric ward.
text: Should the hospital accept the donation? Discuss the ethical considerations.

material: You discover that a colleague in medical school has been fabricating data in their research project. This research is being submitted for publication and may be used to inform clinical practice.
text: What steps would you take? What are the potential consequences of acting or not acting?
Tell me about a time when you had to work with someone whose values or beliefs were very different from your own. How did you find common ground?

material: A 17-year-old athlete is brought to the ER with a knee injury. During examination, you notice bruises on their arms and torso that are inconsistent with a sports injury. The patient's parent is in the room and is answering all questions on the patient's behalf.
text: How would you handle this situation?

material: A 16-year-old patient has been diagnosed with a treatable form of cancer. The patient's parents want to pursue alternative medicine treatments instead of chemotherapy. The patient tells you privately that they want conventional treatment.
text: How would you approach this situation? What are the ethical considerations at play?

material: You are a medical student on a clinical rotation. You witness an attending physician make a medication error that could harm the patient, but the physician does not seem to notice.
text: What would you do in this situation? Walk the interviewer through your reasoning.
What does professionalism mean to you in the context of medicine, and can you give an example of when you demonstrated it?

material: A patient who is a recent immigrant speaks very limited English. They have come to the emergency department with abdominal pain but cannot clearly communicate their symptoms. No interpreter is immediately available.
text: How would you handle this situation? What steps would you take to ensure appropriate care?

material: The government is considering implementing a tax on sugary beverages to combat the obesity epidemic. Proponents argue it will reduce consumption and fund public health programs. Opponents say it disproportionately affects low-income populations.
text: Discuss both sides of this issue. Where do you stand and why?
Describe a time when you showed resilience in the face of a personal or academic challenge.

material: You are a physician and your patient, a 45-year-old woman, asks you to prescribe her a medication she read about online. You do not believe it is medically indicated for her condition, but she is insistent and threatens to find another doctor.
text: How do you handle this interaction? What principles guide your response?

material: A classmate in your medical school cohort has been posting unprofessional content on social media, including mocking patients without using names. Other students have noticed but no one has said anything.
text: What would you do? What are the potential consequences of acting or not acting?
Tell me about a time you worked with someone from a very different cultural background. What did you learn?

material: You are a family physician. A mother brings in her 8-year-old child for a routine checkup. During the visit, the child seems unusually withdrawn and you notice several bruises on the child's arms and back. The mother explains the child fell off a bicycle.
text: What are your responsibilities in this situation? How would you proceed?
What do you think is the most important quality a physician can have, and how do you embody that quality?

material: A hospital is facing budget cuts and must decide between funding a new neonatal intensive care unit or expanding its geriatric care program. Both serve vulnerable populations with significant unmet needs.
text: How would you approach this resource allocation decision? What factors should be considered?

material: You are working in a rural clinic. A patient with a chronic condition needs a specialist referral, but the nearest specialist is 200 miles away and the patient has no reliable transportation or insurance.
text: How would you approach this patient's care? What systemic issues does this scenario highlight?
Describe a situation where you had to make a difficult decision quickly. What was the outcome?

material: A 70-year-old patient with early-stage dementia wants to continue driving. Their family has asked you to tell the patient they can no longer drive. The patient insists they are perfectly capable.
text: How do you balance patient autonomy with public safety in this scenario?

material: Your medical school is considering a policy that would require all students to receive the annual influenza vaccine or be excluded from clinical rotations. Some students object on personal or religious grounds.
text: What is your position on this policy? How do you balance individual rights with public health obligations?
Tell me about a time you had to admit you were wrong. How did you handle it?

material: A pregnant woman at 20 weeks gestation is diagnosed with a condition that poses a moderate risk to her health if the pregnancy continues. She wants to continue the pregnancy despite the risk. Her partner disagrees and wants her to terminate.
text: What are the ethical considerations here? How would you counsel this patient?

material: Your close friend and fellow medical student, James, age 26, confides in you late one evening that he has been struggling with severe depression for the past six months. He has been self-medicating with prescription opioids that he took from a hospital supply cabinet during his clinical rotation in the emergency department. He says the drugs are the only thing that help him sleep. James is currently on a paediatrics rotation and is responsible for patient care. He begs you not to tell anyone, saying that reporting it would end his medical career — and he is $280,000 in student debt. He promises to stop after exams, which are three weeks away. His hands are trembling as he talks to you.
text: The interviewer will play your friend. Engage in this conversation.
How do you define empathy versus sympathy, and why is the distinction important in medicine?

material: A hospital implements a policy requiring physicians to disclose all medical errors to patients, even minor ones that did not cause harm. Some physicians argue this will increase malpractice lawsuits and erode patient trust.
text: Do you support this policy? What are the arguments for and against full disclosure?

material: You are a physician treating a patient who is an undocumented immigrant. A law enforcement officer arrives and asks you for information about the patient's identity and immigration status.
text: How do you respond? What ethical and legal obligations are in tension here?
What experience have you had with the healthcare system as a patient or caregiver, and how has it shaped your desire to become a physician?

material: A 25-year-old patient requests that you not record their HIV-positive status in their medical chart because they are worried about discrimination. They are sexually active and have not disclosed their status to their partner.
text: How do you handle this request? What are your obligations to the patient and to third parties?

material: A new gene therapy can cure a rare childhood disease, but it costs $2.1 million per treatment. The family's insurance refuses to cover it. The hospital has a charity fund, but using it for one patient would deplete resources that could help dozens of others.
text: How should this situation be resolved? What principles of justice apply?
Describe a time you had to communicate sensitive information to someone. How did you approach the conversation?

material: You are a physician and a close family member asks you to prescribe them a medication for a non-urgent condition to save them the cost and inconvenience of seeing another doctor.
text: How do you respond? What are the ethical concerns with treating family members?

material: A community is experiencing a cluster of a rare cancer near an industrial plant. Residents want answers and are demanding the local hospital investigate. The plant is a major employer and donor to the hospital.
text: As a physician at this hospital, how would you approach this situation?
What does cultural competence mean to you, and how would you apply it in clinical practice?

material: A patient with a terminal illness has decided to stop all treatment and pursue medical assistance in dying (MAID), which is legal in your jurisdiction. The patient's adult children are opposed and are pleading with you to convince their parent to continue treatment.
text: How do you navigate this situation? What are the competing ethical principles?

material: You are on a team-based exercise in medical school. One team member consistently dominates the discussion, interrupts others, and dismisses ideas from quieter members of the group.
text: How would you address this dynamic? What leadership skills would you employ?
Tell me about a time you witnessed an injustice. What did you do about it?

material: A healthy 30-year-old patient asks you to order a full-body MRI as a preventive screening. There is no clinical indication. The patient is willing to pay out of pocket.
text: Would you order the scan? What are the potential benefits and harms of this approach?

material: Role-play: You are a medical student on a surgical rotation. The chief resident asks you to perform a procedure on an unconscious patient that you have only observed once and do not feel competent to perform.
text: The interviewer will play the chief resident. Engage in this conversation.
How do you manage stress and prevent burnout, and why is this important for a career in medicine?

material: A local school board is debating whether to implement comprehensive sex education in middle schools. Parents are divided, with some supporting evidence-based education and others citing moral or religious objections.
text: What is your perspective on this issue? How does public health evidence inform your view?

material: A patient requests that only physicians of a specific gender or ethnic background treat them. The request is based on personal preference, not a medical need.
text: How should the healthcare team respond to this request? Where do patient preferences end and discrimination begin?
Describe a time you had to balance your own needs with the needs of others. How did you decide what to do?

material: You discover that a widely used medical device has a small but statistically significant failure rate that the manufacturer has not publicly disclosed. You have patients who use this device.
text: What steps would you take? Who are the stakeholders you need to consider?

material: A 15-year-old patient tells you they are transgender and want to begin hormone therapy. Their parents are unaware of their gender identity and the patient does not want them informed.
text: How would you approach this situation? What are the relevant ethical and legal considerations?
What role do you think social determinants of health play in patient outcomes, and how should physicians address them?

material: During a global health elective in a low-resource country, you observe local physicians practicing in ways that would not meet the standard of care in your home country but are the best available given their resources.
text: How do you reconcile different standards of care across settings? What is your role as a visiting student?

material: A hospital is considering implementing an artificial intelligence system to assist with diagnostic decisions. Some physicians worry it will reduce their autonomy and could introduce algorithmic bias, while administrators cite improved accuracy and efficiency.
text: What is your view on the role of AI in clinical decision-making?
Tell me about a time you had to advocate for yourself in a difficult situation.

material: Role-play: You are a physician delivering a new diagnosis of Type 2 diabetes to a patient who is visibly upset and in denial. The patient says they do not believe in taking medications and wants to manage everything with diet alone.
text: The interviewer will play the patient. Engage in this conversation.

material: A medical school classmate confides that they cheated on an exam. They are at the top of the class and are applying to a highly competitive specialty. They tell you it was a one-time mistake and beg you not to report it.
text: What would you do? What values are in conflict?
What do you think physicians can do to address health misinformation in their communities?

material: An elderly patient in a nursing home has an advance directive stating they do not want CPR. During your shift, the patient goes into cardiac arrest. The patient's daughter, who has just arrived, demands that you resuscitate her parent.
text: How do you handle this situation in real time? What legal and ethical frameworks guide your decision?

material: You are a physician volunteering at a free clinic. You suspect a patient is selling the prescription painkillers you have been prescribing them. The patient denies it and says they need the medication for legitimate pain.
text: How would you approach this situation? What are your responsibilities?
How has a failure or disappointment in your life made you a better candidate for medical school?

material: A government proposes mandatory genetic screening for all newborns to identify predispositions to certain diseases. The data would be stored in a national database for research purposes. Privacy advocates are strongly opposed.
text: Discuss the ethical implications of this policy. What safeguards, if any, would make it acceptable?
What do you think is the greatest challenge facing the medical profession today, and how would you contribute to addressing it?

material: A colleague in your residency program makes a culturally insensitive comment about a patient's religious dietary practices in the break room. Other residents laugh. The patient is not present.
text: How do you respond in this moment? Why does it matter even though the patient is not present?

material: A couple undergoing IVF requests that their embryos be screened not only for genetic diseases but also for traits like eye color and athletic potential. The technology exists to do so.
text: What are the ethical boundaries of genetic selection in reproductive medicine? Where would you draw the line?
Describe a time when you had to show compassion to someone who was difficult to like or agree with.

material: You are a physician in a small town. A patient you are treating for depression is also your child's school teacher. During a parent-teacher conference, the teacher appears to be struggling emotionally.
text: How do you navigate the dual relationship? What boundaries need to be maintained?

material: A hospital's emergency department is consistently overcrowded, leading to long wait times and patients leaving without being seen. The administration proposes turning away non-emergency cases and redirecting them to urgent care clinics.
text: Evaluate this proposal. What are the ethical and practical considerations?
How do you think a physician's personal biases can affect patient care, and what steps can you take to mitigate this?

material: A patient who is a professional athlete asks you to clear them to return to play after a concussion. You do not believe they have fully recovered. The team physician disagrees with your assessment and says the athlete is fine.
text: How do you handle this disagreement? What is your primary obligation?
